diagrams.title: Network Types Hierarchy
diagrams.description: A hierarchical diagram showing the progression from smallest to largest network types: PAN (1-10m) → LAN (building) → MAN (city) → WAN (country/global). Each type labeled with typical speed ranges and examples.
elements: LAN: Office WiFi, Ethernet (building, up to 10 Gbps)
MAN: Campus network, cable TV (city, 100 Mbps - 10 Gbps)
diagrams.purpose: Visualize network scale progression and understand appropriate use cases for each network type

diagrams.title: Network Topologies Comparison
diagrams.description: Six topology diagrams arranged in 2x3 grid: Bus (single backbone line with drops), Star (central hub with spokes), Ring (circular connections), Mesh (interconnected web), Tree (hierarchical), Hybrid (combination). Each shows node connections and communication paths.
elements: Bus: Single cable backbone, terminators at ends
Star: Central device connected to all nodes
Ring: Each node connects to two neighbors forming circle
Mesh: Every node connects to multiple others
Tree: Hierarchical with root and branches
Hybrid: Star backbone with ring or mesh segments
diagrams.purpose: Compare physical arrangements and understand redundancy/cost tradeoffs of each topology

diagrams.title: Latency Components Breakdown
diagrams.description: A timeline diagram showing four delay components when a packet travels from Source → Router1 → Router2 → Destination. Each segment labeled with delay type: Propagation (distance/speed), Transmission (packet size/bandwidth), Processing (header examination), Queuing (buffer wait).
elements: Source to Router1: Propagation + Transmission + Processing + Queuing
Router1 to Router2: Propagation + Transmission + Processing + Queuing
Router2 to Destination: Propagation + Transmission + Processing
Total Latency = Sum of all four delays at each hop
diagrams.purpose: Understand that latency is cumulative across network path and identify factors affecting each component
